Part 1 - EDA (eda.ipynb)

EXPLORATORY DATA ANALYSIS

Age vs Charges (by Smoking Status)

3 distinct bands: Lower band (non-smokers), middle band (smokers + normal BMI, $15K-$35K), upper band (smokers + high BMI, $35K-$64K). Linear age trend present in each band.
Part 1 - EDA (eda.ipynb)

EXPLORATORY DATA ANALYSIS

BMI vs Charges (by Smoking Status)

Critical finding: BMI alone has weak correlation (r=0.198), but for smokers with BMI>30, costs spike dramatically. This non-linear interaction motivated the creation of the smoker_bmi interaction feature.

Part 2 - Preprocessing

PREPROCESSING (preprocessing.py)

Outlier Strategy: IQR Clipping

Outlier detection and clipping using the IQR (Interquartile Range) method:

FeatureOutliersLower BoundUpper BoundAction
age0-9.0087.00No change
bmi913.7047.29Clipped
children0-3.005.00No change
charges———NOT clipped

Why Clipping (Not Removal)?

  • ✔ No data loss — all 1,338 records preserved
  • ✔ Extreme values pulled to boundary limits
  • ! Charges untouched — real high-cost cases
Design decision: Target variable "outliers" are actually real costs of smoker + obese patients. Clipping them would prevent the model from learning this critical group.
Part 2 - Preprocessing

ARCHITECTURAL APPROACH

Model-Specific Pipeline Design

Linear Model Pipeline

1 Outlier Clipping (IQR)
2 Label + One-Hot Encoding
3 StandardScaler (age, bmi, children)
4 Interaction Features (smoker_bmi, smoker_age)
5 Target: log1p(charges)

Skewness: 1.52 → -0.12

Boosting Model Pipeline

1 Outlier Clipping (IQR)
2 Label + One-Hot Encoding
3 StandardScaler (age, bmi, children)
4 Interaction Features (smoker_bmi, smoker_age)
5 Target: RAW charges

Trees handle skewness natively

Why different pipelines? Log transform improved linear R² from 0.78→0.84, but in boosting models, expm1 back-transformation amplified errors at high values, degrading performance. We chose a model-family-specific approach instead of a one-size-fits-all pipeline.
Part 2 - Our Problem

PROBLEM DEFINITION

Insurance Cost Prediction

Predict annual insurance charges based on demographic and health factors.

  • ! Why it matters: Fair pricing for insurers, cost awareness for policyholders
  • ! Challenge: Non-linear interactions between features (smoker × BMI)
  • ! Approach: Compare linear vs ensemble models with feature engineering
3.8x

Smokers pay 3.8x more

$32,050 vs $8,434 average

Cluster 1

Low

Critical finding: The smoker × BMI interaction creates non-linear cost clusters. This directly motivated our feature engineering strategy (interaction terms) and model selection (tree-based ensembles).
Part 3 - EDA

EXPLORATORY DATA ANALYSIS

Smoker Impact Analysis

Non-Smokers (79.5%)

$8,434

Average annual cost
1,064 records

Smokers (20.5%)

$32,050

Average annual cost
274 records

3.8x cost multiplier. Despite being only 20.5% of the dataset, smokers drive the majority of high-cost predictions. This class imbalance makes it crucial for models to learn the smoker-cost relationship accurately.
Part 3 - EDA

EXPLORATORY DATA ANALYSIS

EDA Summary & Conclusions

  • 1 Smoker = #1 predictor — r=0.79, 3.8x cost multiplier, 82.6% feature importance
  • 2 3 cost clusters — driven by smoker × BMI non-linear interaction
  • 3 Age is secondary — clear linear trend within each cluster (r=0.30)
  • 4 Region & sex negligible — combined less than 3% importance
  • 5 Target is skewed — skewness=1.52, needs log transform for linear models
  • 6 Clean data — 0 missing values, no duplicates

Part 4 - Preprocessing

DATA PREPROCESSING

Outlier Detection (IQR Method)

FeatureOutliers FoundLower BoundUpper BoundAction
age0-9.0087.00No change
bmi913.7047.29Clipped
children0-3.005.00No change
charges———NOT clipped
Design decision: Target variable (charges) was NOT clipped. Extreme costs represent real high-risk cases the model must learn to predict. Clipping would cap predictions and degrade performance.

Part 5 - Feature Engineering

FEATURE ENGINEERING

Interaction Features (V2)

EDA revealed non-linear clusters. We create explicit interaction terms:

smoker × BMI

Captures the synergy between smoking and obesity that produces disproportionately high costs

smoker × Age

Captures the accelerated cost increase for smokers as they age

Feature Space

8 features → 10 features
Impact: Linear R² jumped from 0.78 to 0.84 (+6%). Boosting models gained ~1% (they learn interactions implicitly through tree splits).
Part 5 - Feature Engineering

FEATURE ENGINEERING

Hybrid Log Transform Strategy

Linear Models ✔

Target: np.log1p(charges)

Skewness: 1.52 -> -0.12 | R2: 0.78 -> 0.84

Back-transform: np.expm1(prediction)

Boosting Models ✘

Target: raw charges

Trees handle skewness natively

expm1 back-transform amplifies errors at high values

Part 5b - Engineering

ENGINEERING DECISIONS

RandomizedSearchCV Strategy

Used Randomized Search instead of Grid Search:

XGBoost Search Space

ParameterRange
n_estimators[200, 300, 500, 800, 1000]
max_depth[3, 4, 5, 6, 7]
learning_rate[0.01, 0.03, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2]
subsample[0.7, 0.8, 0.9, 1.0]
colsample_bytree[0.7, 0.8, 0.9, 1.0]
reg_alpha (L1)[0, 0.1, 0.5, 1.0]
reg_lambda (L2)[1, 1.5, 2, 3]

Iterations

40

per model

CV Fold

5

cross-validation

Total Fits

600

3 model x 40 x 5

Model-Specific Parameters

  • XGB colsample_bytree (column sampling)
  • LGBM num_leaves [15-100] (leaf-based splitting)
  • GBR min_samples_split [2,5,10]
n_jobs=-1 uses all CPU cores in parallel. Total search space: ~112K combinations, efficiently explored with 40 random samples.
Part 5b - Engineering

ENGINEERING DECISIONS

4-Layer Overfitting Protection

Multi-layer regularization strategy for boosting models:

  • 1 L1 (reg_alpha) — Drives insignificant feature splits to zero, enforcing sparsity
  • 2 L2 (reg_lambda) — Shrinks leaf weights to prevent overfitting
  • 3 Row Subsampling — Each tree uses 70-90% of data, reducing variance
  • 4 Column Subsampling — Each tree sees 70-90% of features, decorrelating trees

XGBoost Optimal Parameters

n_estimators500
max_depth3 (shallow trees)
learning_rate0.01
subsample0.8
colsample_bytree0.9
max_depth=3 critical choice: 500 shallow trees instead of deep ones. Each tree acts as a "weak learner", strengthened through boosting.
Part 5b - Engineering

ENGINEERING DECISIONS

Safety & Robustness

Negative Prediction Guard

np.maximum(pred, 0)

Cost cannot be negative — all predictions clipped to 0

What-If Filtering

if savings > 0

Only positive savings scenarios are shown

Graceful Degradation

len(preds) >= 2

Min 2 models required for confidence — fallback on error

Reproducibility

random_state=42

Fixed seed across all operations — reproducible results

Hard-coded Scaler

SCALER = {mean, std}

Removes joblib dependency in web app for simpler deployment

API Fallback

if not API_KEY

Predictions still work without Claude API — AI is optional

Part 6 - Modeling

MODEL TRAINING

5 Models Trained & Compared

Baseline (Linear)

  • 1 Linear Regression
  • 2 Ridge Regression (L2)

Ensemble (Boosting)

  • 3 XGBoost
  • 4 LightGBM
  • 5 GradientBoosting

Hyperparameter Optimization

  • RandomizedSearchCV
  • 5-fold cross-validation
  • 40 random iterations per model
  • Scoring metric: R²
  • Parameters: n_estimators, max_depth, learning_rate, subsample, regularization
Part 6 - Modeling

MODEL TRAINING

XGBoost - How It Works

Extreme Gradient Boosting (Chen & Guestrin, 2016):

  • 1 Builds trees sequentially, each correcting errors of the previous
  • 2 Uses gradient descent to minimize loss function
  • 3 Built-in L1 + L2 regularization to prevent overfitting
  • 4 Column subsampling for additional regularization

Best Parameters Found

ParamValue
n_estimators500
max_depth3
learning_rate0.01
subsample0.8
colsample_bytree0.9

Part 8 - Explainable AI

EXPLAINABLE AI

What is SHAP?

SHapley Additive exPlanations (Lundberg & Lee, 2017)

  • ■ Based on Shapley values from game theory
  • ■ Decomposes prediction into per-feature contributions
  • ■ Satisfies local accuracy, consistency, missingness
  • ■ Works with any ML model (we use TreeExplainer)

Formula

f(x) = E[f(x)] + Σ φᵢ

Where φᵢ is the SHAP value for feature i — its marginal contribution to the prediction relative to the expected value.

Part 8 - Explainable AI

EXPLAINABLE AI

Per-Prediction SHAP Breakdown

Each user sees exactly why their cost is what it is:

Smoker
+$12,755
Smoker×BMI
-$3,762
Age
-$2,593
BMI
-$1,845
Increases cost Decreases cost
Top 2 SHAP features are dynamically fed to Claude AI for personalized advice generation.
Part 8 - Explainable AI

EXPLAINABLE AI

Why Explainability Matters

  • 1 Trust — Users need to understand why the model predicts a certain cost
  • 2 Fairness — Ensures model isn't biased against protected groups
  • 3 Actionability — "Your smoking increases cost by $12K" is actionable; "$25K predicted" is not
  • 4 Debugging — Reveals if the model is using features correctly
  • 5 Regulation — EU AI Act and similar regulations require explainability for high-stakes decisions

Part 9 - Deployment

WEB APPLICATION

Smart Insurance Advisor V2.0

  • 1 Dataset Dashboard — Animated stat cards + model comparison on welcome screen
  • 2 BMI Auto-Calculator — Height/weight inputs with color-coded BMI category
  • 3 Real-Time Prediction — Animated cost counter with 3-model confidence interval
  • 4 What-If Analysis — "Quit smoking" and "Lower BMI" scenario simulation
  • 5 SHAP Breakdown — Animated CSS bar chart per prediction
  • 6 Claude AI Report — Personalized health optimization advice
  • 7 Report Download — Export as markdown file
Part 9 - Deployment

WEB APPLICATION

What-If Scenario Engine

If You Quit Smoking

Model re-runs with smoker=0
$8,240

Save $23,810/year

If BMI Reaches 25

Model re-runs with bmi=25
$27,500

Save $4,550/year

Safety feature: Scenarios with negative savings (where the "improvement" actually increases cost due to non-linear model behavior) are automatically filtered out to prevent misleading advice.
Part 9 - Deployment

WEB APPLICATION

Claude AI Integration

Generative AI transforms numbers into actionable advice:

  • 1 SHAP top-2 features dynamically injected into prompt
  • 2 Savings scenarios with specific dollar amounts included
  • 3 AI generates empathetic, personalized report
  • 4 Report downloadable as markdown file

Sample AI Output

"Our AI analysis shows that smoking status is the primary driver of your insurance cost. By quitting smoking, you could save $23,810 per year. Here are some actionable steps..."

Part 9 - Deployment

WEB APPLICATION

Prediction Confidence Interval

Three boosting models provide independent estimates:

ModelPrediction
XGBoost$32,050
LightGBM$31,200
GradientBoosting$33,100

Low

$31,200

Average

$32,117

High

$33,100
No probabilistic assumptions needed. The spread of 3 independent model predictions provides a practical confidence band.
Part 9 - Deployment

WEB APPLICATION

UI Design Philosophy

  • ■ Custom dark theme — Glassmorphism, gradient backgrounds, Inter font
  • ■ Zero framework dependency — Pure HTML/CSS/JS, no Streamlit/Bootstrap
  • ■ Animations — Cost counter animates, SHAP bars grow with staggered timing
  • ■ Responsive — Works on desktop and mobile
  • ■ Auto-open — `python app.py` opens browser automatically
Part 10 - Conclusion

CONCLUSION

Key Takeaways

  • 1 Smoking = #1 driver — 82.6% importance, 3.8x cost multiplier
  • 2 Non-linear interactions — smoker×BMI creates distinct cost clusters
  • 3 XGBoost best model — R²=0.88, outperforms linear by 5%
  • 4 Feature engineering works — Linear R² from 0.78 to 0.84
  • 5 Log transform is model-dependent — helps linear, hurts boosting
  • 6 SHAP = transparency — every prediction is explainable
  • 7 AI adds value — Claude turns numbers into actionable advice
Part 10 - Conclusion

FUTURE WORK

Potential Improvements

  • 1 Stacking Ensemble — Meta-learner combining all 3 boosting models (target: R² > 0.90)
  • 2 Larger Dataset — Real-world data with 100K+ records for better generalization
  • 3 Temporal Features — Year-over-year health changes for improved accuracy
  • 4 Fairness Analysis — Bias evaluation across sex and region
  • 5 Probabilistic Predictions — Quantile regression for calibrated confidence intervals
  • 6 Deep Learning — TabNet or FT-Transformer comparison
Part 10 - Conclusion

TECHNOLOGIES

Tech Stack Summary

Data Analysis

Python 3.13
pandas, numpy
matplotlib, seaborn
Jupyter Notebook

Machine Learning

scikit-learn
XGBoost
LightGBM
SHAP

Deployment

Flask
HTML5/CSS3/JS
Claude Haiku API
joblib

System Design

ARCHITECTURE

Complete System Architecture

4-layer architecture: User Interface (Web/CSV/API) -> Flask Application (Routes, Feature Builder, Safety) -> ML Layer (5 models + SHAP + Claude) -> Data Layer (CSV, scaler, preprocessed splits). DevOps (Docker + CI/CD + pytest) supports the stack.
Part 7 - Results

RESULTS

Residuals vs Predicted (Homoskedasticity)

Approximately homoskedastic: residuals have relatively constant variance across predicted values. Most errors fall within +-1 sigma (~$4,300). Slightly increasing spread at high predictions reveals the model is less confident in extreme cost regions.
Part 7 - Results

RESULTS

Prediction Accuracy by Cost Range

Best Range

$30K-$50K

Only 9.5% error

Weakest Range

$0-$5K

55% error (small absolute amounts)

Sweet Spot

$10K-$20K

13.8% error, 56 test samples

Part 3 - EDA

EXPLORATORY DATA ANALYSIS

Cost Distribution per Region

Regional similarity: All four US regions show nearly identical cost distributions (left). When split by smoking status (right), the smoker-vs-nonsmoker gap dominates regional differences. This confirms region's low correlation (r~0.02) with charges.
Part 9 - Web App V3

NEW FEATURE

Batch CSV Prediction

Upload a CSV with multiple records, get predictions + SHAP insights for each row:

  • 1 CSV Upload — required columns: age, sex, bmi, children, smoker, region
  • 2 Per-row Prediction — XGBoost price estimate
  • 3 Per-row SHAP — top contributing feature identified for each case
  • 4 Summary Statistics — total, success, mean/min/max predictions
  • 5 Downloadable CSV — full results exportable as CSV

POST /batch_predict Endpoint

file = open('clients.csv', 'rb')
response = requests.post(
    'http://api/batch_predict',
    files={'file': file}
)
# Returns: JSON with per-row
# predictions + top SHAP features
# Add ?format=csv for CSV export

Error handling: missing columns -> 400, invalid types -> row-level error with other rows still processed. Graceful degradation.

Part 9 - Web App V3

NEW FEATURE

Similar Patients (KNN)

K-Nearest Neighbors module finds the 5 most similar historical patients from the training set:

  • 1 Metric — Euclidean distance on 8 scaled features
  • 2 K=5 — returns 5 nearest training records
  • 3 Actual charges — shows what these real patients actually paid
  • 4 Similarity % — heuristic score based on distance
  • 5 Summary stats — min/mean/median/max of similar patient costs
Why valuable? Users see "patients with your profile actually paid $X-$Y" as a sanity check beyond model predictions. Builds trust through evidence.

Implementation

from sklearn.neighbors import NearestNeighbors

knn = NearestNeighbors(
    n_neighbors=5,
    metric='euclidean'
)
knn.fit(X_train_scaled)

# At prediction time:
distances, indices = knn.kneighbors(
    user_feature_vector
)
# Returns actual charges from
# training set rows

Fit once at app startup. Query time: ~2ms for 5 neighbors on 1,070 training samples.

Part 10 - DevOps

DEVOPS

Docker Containerization

Production-ready Docker setup for one-command deployment:

FROM python:3.11-slim

RUN apt-get install -y libgomp1

WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install -r requirements.txt

COPY . .
EXPOSE 5000

HEALTHCHECK CMD python -c "urllib.request
    .urlopen('http://localhost:5000/api/stats')"

CMD ["python", "app.py"]

Deploy with Compose

$ docker-compose up -d

One command, full stack up.

Image Layers

  • 1 python:3.11-slim base (~150MB)
  • 2 libgomp1 (XGBoost/LightGBM)
  • 3 requirements cached layer
  • 4 app code layer

Health check every 30s. Auto-restart on failure.

Part 10 - DevOps

DEVOPS

GitHub Actions CI/CD

3-job pipeline runs on every push and PR:

Job 1: Test

  • - Python matrix: 3.11, 3.12
  • - flake8 lint (syntax errors only)
  • - pytest with coverage
  • - Codecov upload

Job 2: Docker Build

  • - Docker buildx with cache
  • - Verifies image builds cleanly
  • - Image size reporting

Job 3: Data Integrity

  • - Dataset row count check (1,338)
  • - All 5 models load correctly
  • - Zero missing values assertion

Test Results

tests/test_preprocessing.py .......  [32%]
tests/test_models.py ..........    [63%]
tests/test_app_endpoints.py .....  [100%]

======== 22 passed in 27s ========

Coverage: 87%
Green checkmark on every commit means: all tests pass, Docker builds, data is intact. Catches regressions before they ship.
